text: Percentage of heart rate reserve (%HRR) is equivalent to percentage of VO2 reserve (%VO2R), not to percentage of VO2max, with a regression intercept of -0.1 and slope of 1.00 (indistinguishable from the line of identity).

Using %HRR as equivalent to %VO2max systematically overestimates exercise intensity, with a mean regression intercept of -11.6 and slope of 1.12, both significantly different from the line of identity (P < 0.001).
The discrepancy between %HRR and %VO2max is inversely proportional to fitness level — less fit individuals experience a larger overestimation of exercise intensity when %HRR is equated to %VO2max.
Exercise intensity prescription using heart rate should use the Karvonen (heart rate reserve) method with %VO2R equivalence rather than %VO2max equivalence for accurate intensity targeting.
VO2 reserve is defined as the difference between resting VO2 and maximal VO2, and is the correct metabolic equivalent of heart rate reserve (the difference between resting HR and maximal HR).
